kings-gambit.annotation: The knight leaps to the powerful e5 outpost rather than retreating! From e5, the knight attacks f7, d7, and g4, creating multiple threats. This is the Kieseritzky Gambit's signature move — White sacrifices another pawn (h4 is hanging) for a devastating central knight and blistering piece activity.
plans: Attack f7 — Black's weakest point
Coordinate Bc4 + Ne5 for maximum pressure on f7
The knight on e5 is worth more than the pawns Black holds
alternatives: Ng1 — retreating is too slow, Black consolidates easily
Ng5 — aggressive but less central than e5

kings-gambit.annotation: Black develops the knight to defend while counterattacking e4. The knight on f6 is doing critical work — it defends h5, controls d5, and attacks the e4 pawn. This is the most principled developing move, keeping Black in the game while White's attack builds.
plans: Defend the kingside while developing
Attack e4 to create counterplay
alternatives: d6 — kicks the knight but is slower on development
Bg7 — develops the bishop but doesn't help with e4 pressure

kings-gambit.annotation: White trains the bishop on f7 — now both the knight and bishop converge on Black's most vulnerable point. The combination of Bc4 + Ne5 creates immense tactical pressure. This is the classic setup in the King's Gambit: every piece aims at the black king's neighborhood.
plans: Double attack on f7 with bishop and knight
Prepare O-O to bring the rook into the attack via the f-file
Look for Bxf7+ or Nxf7 tactical shots
alternatives: d4 — builds the center but doesn't increase pressure on f7 as directly

kings-gambit.annotation: Black strikes back in the center with a counter-sacrifice! By pushing d5, Black attacks both the bishop on c4 and the pawn on e4, breaking up White's center. This is the key defensive resource — rather than passively suffering, Black opens lines for the bishops and challenges White to prove the attack is worth more than material.
kings-gambit.pawnStructure: The d5 break changes the entire pawn structure. After exd5, the center opens completely and both kings are in danger. The game becomes a race between White's kingside attack and Black's central counterplay.
plans: Break open the center to activate the bishops
Challenge White's attacking setup by creating counterplay
alternatives: Be7 — solid but passive, White builds up with O-O and d4 easily

kings-gambit.annotation: White captures, opening the e-file and maintaining the bishop on c4. The d5 pawn is now a passed pawn which could become relevant later. More importantly, the position is now completely open — tactical fireworks are inevitable with both kings exposed.
kings-gambit.pawnStructure: White has a passed d5 pawn and open central lines. The open e-file and the bishop on c4 create a deadly battery.
plans: Use the passed d-pawn as a long-term asset
Maintain pressure on f7 with Bc4 and Ne5
alternatives: Bb3 — retreats but loses the central tension

kings-gambit.annotation: Black develops the bishop to an excellent attacking and defensive square. On d6, the bishop attacks the powerful e5 knight while also supporting the f4 pawn. If the knight is driven away, Black's bishop will dominate the long diagonal. This is a multipurpose move that addresses both attack and defense.
plans: Challenge the e5 knight
Support the f4 pawn and maintain the kingside pawn chain
alternatives: Nh5 — aggressive but leaves the kingside uncoordinated
Bg7 — develops but doesn't challenge e5

kings-gambit.annotation: White builds a massive pawn center with d4, supporting the e5 knight and gaining space. The d4-d5 pawn duo is extremely powerful, restricting Black's pieces. White's center now looks like a steamroller — combined with the attacking pieces, the position is razor-sharp.
kings-gambit.pawnStructure: White's d4-d5 pawn center is imposing, grabbing space on both sides. The passed d5 pawn can become a monster in the endgame.
plans: Support the e5 knight permanently
Use the d5 passed pawn as a long-term weapon
Castle and bring the rook to the f-file
alternatives: O-O — castling is tempting but d4 solidifies the center first

kings-gambit.annotation: The knight jumps to h5 with aggressive intent — eyeing f4 to recapture the pawn with the knight, or potentially reaching g3. On h5, the knight also eyes f4 and g3, creating threats against White's king if it castles kingside. This is a creative attempt to generate counterplay.
plans: Target f4 with the knight for a powerful outpost
Create threats against White's kingside if it castles
alternatives: O-O — castling is reasonable but the position demands active play
Nd7 — developing but blocking the c8 bishop

kings-gambit.annotation: White castles into the storm! This is the whole point of the King's Gambit — the rook immediately comes to f1, staring down the open f-file at Black's king. Even though Black has pawns on f4 and g4 threatening the kingside, White's pieces are so active that the attack plays itself.
plans: Activate the rook on f1 along the open f-file
Look for Rxf4 or Nxf7 sacrificial breakthroughs
The connected rooks and centralized pieces create overwhelming pressure
alternatives: Nd2 — slow, White should castle and attack immediately

kings-gambit.annotation: Black grabs the h4 pawn with the queen, gaining material and creating threats along the h-file. The queen on h4 is actively placed, eyeing f2 and the white king. However, the queen is also somewhat exposed here, and White's piece activity and open f-file provide serious compensation. This position is one of the most critical battlegrounds in King's Gambit theory.
plans: Use the queen's active position to threaten White's king
Coordinate queen + knight on h5 for kingside pressure
alternatives: Bxe5 — captures the knight but dxe5 gives White a protected passed pawn and open lines

kings-indian-attack.annotation: We start with the knight to f3, which is the most flexible first move in chess. Unlike 1.e4 or 1.d4, this doesn't commit to a pawn structure yet -- we're saying 'I'm going to develop sensibly and choose my structure based on what you do.' This is the hallmark of the King's Indian Attack: we have a system, and we're going to play it regardless of Black's setup.
plans: Fianchetto the king's bishop to g2
Transpose into a KIA structure with d3, Nbd2, e4
alternatives: d4 - enters mainline queen's pawn openings
g3 - reaches the same setup but tips your hand earlier

kings-indian-attack.annotation: Black grabs the center immediately with the d-pawn. This is the most principled response -- occupying d5 while it's uncontested. Black is essentially saying 'if you're not going to fight for the center with pawns right away, I will.' This gives Black a classical pawn center and good piece development prospects.
kings-indian-attack.pawnStructure: Black establishes a d5 outpost, aiming for a solid central presence.
alternatives: Nf6 - a more flexible approach that mirrors White's strategy
d6 - more passive, allows White easy KIA setup

kings-indian-attack.annotation: Here's the signature KIA move. We're fianchettoing the bishop to g2 where it becomes an absolute monster on the long diagonal. The bishop on g2 will stare down at d5 and the entire queenside, putting constant pressure on Black's center. The g3 pawn also supports a future kingside castle and creates a rock-solid pawn shield.
kings-indian-attack.pawnStructure: The g3-d3 structure forms the backbone of the KIA, creating a flexible but solid formation.
plans: Bg2 completes the fianchetto
Castle kingside quickly for king safety
alternatives: d3 - reaches the same setup but delays the fianchetto

kings-indian-attack.annotation: Black develops the kingside knight to its most natural square, defending d5 and preparing to castle. Nf6 also eyes e4, which is currently unoccupied. This is classical development -- knights before bishops, toward the center. Black is building a solid, flexible position.
alternatives: c6 - reinforces d5 immediately but delays development
Bg4 - pins the knight but can be met with various ideas

kings-indian-attack.annotation: Now our bishop reaches the powerhouse g2 square. Look at that diagonal -- a1 to h8, and crucially it X-rays right through d5. This bishop is going to be a constant source of pressure. Even though it's behind our own pawns right now, once we play e4 and open the center, this bishop becomes a beast. It also provides excellent support for our king once we castle.
plans: Castle immediately
Follow up with d3 and Nbd2 to prepare the e4 break

kings-indian-attack.annotation: Black shores up the d5 pawn with c6, creating a Slav-like structure. This is very solid -- d5 is now defended by both the c6 pawn and the Nf6. Black is saying 'you're not going to undermine my center easily.' The downside is the knight on b8 loses the c6 square, so it'll likely develop to d7 instead.
kings-indian-attack.pawnStructure: The c6-d5 pawn chain is a fortress in the center, characteristic of Slav structures.
alternatives: e6 - creates a French-like structure but locks in the light-squared bishop
Bg4 - active development before committing the pawn structure

kings-indian-attack.annotation: Castle immediately -- get the king safe before we start any central action. This is a KIA principle: we don't rush e4. We tuck the king away, connect the rooks, and then prepare our central break methodically. The king is beautifully sheltered behind the g3-f2 pawn chain with the bishop on g2 providing extra cover.
plans: d3 followed by Nbd2 to prepare e4
Re1 to support the e4 push

kings-indian-attack.annotation: Black pins the f3 knight against the queen, which is a natural developing move. The idea is to create some pressure and potentially trade the bishop for the knight if White plays e4, since after the trade Black would have less pressure on d5. It also develops a piece toward the center and gets ready to castle queenside or kingside.
plans: Maintain pin on Nf3 to slow White's e4 plans
Potentially trade on f3 to double White's pawns
alternatives: Be7 - simple development preparing to castle
Bd6 - more aggressive post but blocks the d-pawn

kings-indian-attack.annotation: We play d3 rather than d4 -- this is what makes it the King's Indian Attack and not a standard opening. The pawn on d3 supports a future e4 push and keeps our structure flexible. d3 also opens the diagonal for our c1 bishop. We're building a coiled spring: once e4 comes, the position opens up and our pieces come alive.
kings-indian-attack.pawnStructure: The d3-e4 structure (once e4 is played) creates a small center that's very hard to attack.
plans: Nbd2 to prepare e4
h3 if needed to challenge the Bg4
alternatives: h3 - immediately challenges the bishop but weakens the kingside slightly

kings-indian-attack.annotation: Black develops the knight to d7 since c6 is taken by the pawn. From d7 the knight supports e5 and can reroute to various squares -- c5, f8, or even b6. It's a flexible placement that doesn't block any of Black's pieces. This is typical in Slav-type structures where the c6 pawn takes the knight's natural square.
plans: Support e5 push to challenge White's center
Reroute the knight to c5 or f8 depending on the position
alternatives: Be7 - simple development, but Nbd7 is more flexible

kings-indian-attack.annotation: The knight goes to d2 rather than c3 -- this is critical KIA technique. On c3, the knight would block our c-pawn and compete with the g2 bishop for influence on d5. On d2, it directly supports the e4 push and can later reroute to f1-e3 or even g5 in attacking positions. Every piece is aimed at making e4 happen.
plans: Play e4 to challenge Black's center
After e4, potentially reroute Nd2 to f1-e3-d5
alternatives: Nc3 - more active but blocks the c-pawn and is less harmonious in the KIA

kings-indian-attack.annotation: Black claims space in the center with e5, establishing a big pawn duo on d5 and e5. This looks impressive but it also creates a target -- the d5 pawn is now potentially weak if White can play e4 and exchange. Black is also committing to a structure where the dark-squared bishop might struggle to find an active diagonal.
kings-indian-attack.pawnStructure: The d5-e5 center is imposing but can become a target after White's e4 break.
plans: Develop the dark-squared bishop to c5 or d6
Maintain the strong center and play for a kingside or queenside attack
alternatives: e6 - more solid but passive, locks in the light-squared bishop

kings-indian-attack.annotation: Finally, the move we've been building toward! e4 strikes at the heart of Black's center. After the exchange on e4, our g2 bishop will come roaring to life on the long diagonal, and we'll have excellent central control. This is the payoff for all that patient preparation -- d3, Nbd2, everything was about making this break work.
kings-indian-attack.pawnStructure: After dxe4 dxe4, White gets a central pawn on e4 with open lines for all pieces.
plans: Recapture on e4 with the d-pawn to open the d-file
Activate the g2 bishop on the opened diagonal

kings-indian-attack.annotation: Black essentially has to take. If Black doesn't capture, White takes on d5 and gets a great position with a passed or advanced e-pawn. By taking dxe4, Black opens the position but also gives White exactly what we wanted -- the d3 pawn recaptures and the g2 bishop lights up. Sometimes you have to accept that your opponent's plan worked and find counterplay elsewhere.
alternatives: d4 - trying to keep the center closed, but after c3 White undermines it anyway

kings-indian-attack.annotation: We recapture with the d-pawn, and now look at the transformation. Our g2 bishop is a monster on the long diagonal, staring at c6 and beyond. We have a solid e4 pawn in the center, our pieces are all developed, and we have clear plans. The position has opened up perfectly for our setup -- this is what KIA dreams are made of.
kings-indian-attack.pawnStructure: White's e4 pawn controls d5 and f5, giving excellent central influence with open diagonals.
plans: Play for a kingside attack with Qe2, Re1
Use the strong e4 pawn as a springboard for piece activity

kings-indian-attack.annotation: Black puts the bishop on c5 where it's actively placed, eyeing f2 and controlling the a7-g1 diagonal. This is the best square for the dark-squared bishop now that e5 is occupied by a pawn. From c5 it also supports a potential d4 push and keeps an eye on the kingside. It's a good practical choice in a position where Black needs active pieces to compensate for the slightly loosened center.
plans: Pressure f2 and the a7-g1 diagonal
Support potential d4 or b5 breaks
alternatives: Be7 - safer but more passive, the bishop does less on e7
Bd6 - eyes the kingside but blocks the d-file

kings-indian-defence.annotation: White opens with the queen's pawn, claiming the center. Against the King's Indian, this move takes on special significance because White is going to try to build a massive pawn center while Black lets them do it, planning to attack it later. The whole KID is a philosophical debate: is a big center a strength or a target?
plans: Build a broad center with c4 and e4
alternatives: e4 -- leads to different openings entirely

kings-indian-defence.annotation: Black plays the Indian move order, developing the knight and controlling e4. Rather than contesting the center with ...d5 immediately, Black says 'go ahead, build your center -- I'll attack it later.' This is the hypermodern approach: let your opponent overextend in the center, then strike at the foundation. Nf6 prevents e4 for now and keeps Black's options completely open.
plans: Play ...g6 and ...Bg7 for the King's Indian setup
Let White build the center, then attack it
alternatives: d5 -- the classical approach, meeting the center head-on

kings-indian-defence.annotation: White grabs more central space, now controlling d5. Together with d4, White has pawns on c4 and d4 which is the ideal setup in Queen's Pawn openings. White is daring Black to allow e4, which would create a truly massive center. The c4 pawn also prepares Nc3, which will add more pressure to the center.
kings-indian-defence.pawnStructure: White's c4+d4 broad center is the foundation of their strategy
plans: Play Nc3 and e4 for maximum central control

kings-indian-defence.annotation: This is the defining move of the King's Indian Defense. Black prepares to fianchetto the bishop on g7, where it becomes a monster aiming at the center and the queenside along the long diagonal. The g6 pawn also creates a solid kingside shelter for castling. Black is deliberately allowing White to build a big center because the Bg7 will put enormous pressure on d4 from the flank.
kings-indian-defence.pawnStructure: Black's kingside fianchetto with g6 creates a fortress but gives White central space
plans: Fianchetto with Bg7, castle, then strike with ...e5 or ...c5
alternatives: d5 -- the Grunfeld, challenging the center immediately with the bishop already committed to flank development

kings-indian-defence.annotation: White develops the knight to c3, controlling d5 and e4. This knight is a critical piece in the KID -- it supports the e4 advance and helps maintain the pawn center. On c3, the knight is beautifully placed to support White's central strategy. The stage is set for e4 on the next move.
plans: Play e4 to build the full center
The knight supports d5 and e4

kings-indian-defence.annotation: Black completes the fianchetto. The bishop on g7 is the pride of Black's position -- it's a sniper aimed along the h8-a1 diagonal, putting indirect pressure on d4. Once Black plays ...e5, this bishop becomes incredibly powerful as it aligns with the center. In many KID lines, this bishop single-handedly holds Black's position together while enabling kingside attacks.
plans: The Bg7 pressures d4 and supports a future ...e5 break
Castle next to complete kingside development
alternatives: Already committed to the KID setup -- this is the only consistent move

kings-indian-defence.annotation: White achieves the dream setup: pawns on c4, d4, and e4. This is a massive center that controls almost every important square. Most players would be terrified to allow this, but King's Indian players welcome it. The theory is that this center is overextended and can be attacked with ...e5 or ...c5. White's center is strong but also a potential liability if Black can undermine it.
kings-indian-defence.pawnStructure: White's c4-d4-e4 pawn center is the maximum possible central presence
plans: Use the space advantage for piece development and queenside expansion
alternatives: This is the main line -- any other move is less ambitious

kings-indian-defence.annotation: Black prepares ...e5, the key central break. The d6 pawn supports e5 and also prevents White from playing e5 themselves. This is a crucial preparatory move -- Black can't play ...e5 without d6 because the pawn would hang. The d6 pawn also keeps the position flexible; Black's setup is incredibly resilient and hard to breach despite giving up so much center.
kings-indian-defence.pawnStructure: Black's d6 pawn supports the upcoming ...e5 central challenge
plans: Play ...e5 to challenge White's center
Castle and complete the KID setup
alternatives: O-O -- castling first is also possible, but d6 is needed before ...e5

kings-indian-defence.annotation: White develops the last minor piece to its natural square. The knight on f3 supports d4 and e4, preparing to castle. White has a textbook classical setup. The question now is how to continue after Black plays ...e5 -- the answer usually involves d5, closing the center and starting a race where White attacks on the queenside and Black attacks on the kingside.
plans: Castle and maintain the center
After ...e5, decide between dxe5, d5, or maintaining tension
alternatives: Be2 -- developing the bishop first, also standard

kings-indian-defence.annotation: Black castles immediately, getting the king safe before the central conflict begins. The king on g8 is well-protected behind the g6-f7 pawns and the Bg7. Crucially, the rook comes to f8, where it will support ...f5, which is one of Black's most important attacking ideas in the KID. Everything in Black's setup is geared toward a kingside attack.
plans: Play ...e5, then ...f5 for a kingside attack
The Rf8 supports the ...f5 break

kings-indian-defence.annotation: White develops the bishop to e2, a solid choice. The bishop doesn't go to the more aggressive d3 because it would block the d-file, and in KID positions the d-file becomes important after d5. On e2, the bishop supports castling, can later swing to f3 or d3 if needed, and keeps the position flexible. It's not flashy, but it's the most reliable square.
plans: Castle next, then decide on a plan based on Black's setup
alternatives: Bd3 -- more active but blocks the d-file in the main line structures

kings-indian-defence.annotation: The key moment! Black strikes at the center with ...e5, challenging White's d4 pawn. This is the move the entire King's Indian is built around. After d5, the position locks up and the game becomes a race: White pushes on the queenside with c5, Black attacks on the kingside with ...f5, ...g5, and eventually ...f4. It's like two armies fighting on different flanks.
kings-indian-defence.pawnStructure: After d5, a locked center creates a classic KID pawn structure with opposite-flank attacks
plans: After d5, launch a kingside attack with ...f5, ...Nf6-h5-f4
The Bg7 comes alive pressuring d4 from behind
alternatives: Nc6 -- developing first before ...e5, but the immediate break is the main line

kings-indian-defence.annotation: White castles before the center closes. Getting the king safe is critical because the game is about to become incredibly sharp. The rook on f1 will also be useful for supporting f3 or swinging to the queenside. White is now ready for d5, which will lock the center and start the classic KID race.
plans: Play d5 to lock the center, then expand on the queenside with c5, a4, b4
alternatives: d5 -- closing the center immediately is also played

kings-indian-defence.annotation: Black develops the knight, adding pressure to d4. This is a key move -- the knight on c6 forces White's hand regarding the center. White almost has to play d5 now, because the d4 pawn is attacked twice and defended only once (after ...exd4, Nxd4 Nxd4 isn't ideal for White). The knight will get kicked to e7 after d5, but from there it reroutes to the kingside for the attack.
plans: Force d5, then reroute the knight via e7 to g6 or f5 for the kingside attack
alternatives: Nbd7 -- keeping the knight out of the way of d5, but Nc6 forces the issue more concretely

kings-indian-defence.annotation: White closes the center, and the classic KID structure is established. This is one of the most theoretically important positions in chess. The center is locked, and the game becomes a race: White will attack on the queenside (c5, a4, b4, Nd3-b5) while Black attacks on the kingside (...f5, ...f4, ...g5, ...Rf6-h6). It's a pure battle of who gets there first.
kings-indian-defence.pawnStructure: Locked center: White's c4-d5-e4 chain vs Black's d6-e5. Opposite-side attacks begin.
plans: Expand on the queenside with c5, a4, b4
Win the race against Black's kingside attack
alternatives: dxe5 -- opening the center is possible but gives Black excellent piece play with the Bg7

kings-indian-defence.annotation: The knight retreats to e7, clearing c6 for other pieces and heading for the kingside. This is the standard KID maneuver -- the knight will go to f5 or g6, or support ...f5 from e7. It looks like a retreat but it's actually redeployment: the knight was doing nothing useful on c6 after d5 blocked the center. On e7, it's a flexible piece ready to jump into the attack.
plans: Reroute to g6, f5, or d7 depending on the position
Support ...f5 from e7
alternatives: Na5 -- attacking c4 immediately, a sideline but less common

kings-indian-defence.annotation: White reroutes the knight from f3. This looks weird but it's a very deep idea. The knight is heading to d3, where it supports both c5 (the queenside advance) and f4 (preventing Black's kingside attack). From d3, the knight is perfectly placed for White's strategic needs. It also clears f3 for the f-pawn, which might need to support e4 with f3 if Black's pieces pressure it.
plans: Reroute Nd3, supporting c5 and controlling f4
Clear f3 for the f-pawn if needed
alternatives: Nd2 -- another regrouping option, heading for c5 via b3

kings-indian-defence.annotation: Black reroutes the knight from f6. This is another classic KID maneuver -- the knight leaves f6 to make room for ...f5, which is the key attacking break. The knight on d7 can go to f6 later (after ...f5) or to c5 to pressure e4. Every piece in Black's army is reorganizing for the kingside assault. The f6 square needs to be clear so the f-pawn can advance.
plans: Clear f6 for the ...f5 break
Reroute Nd7-f6 or Nd7-c5 depending on the position
alternatives: f5 -- playing the break immediately is possible, but Nd7 first improves the knight placement

kings-indian-defence.annotation: White plays f3 to solidify the e4 pawn and create a fortress-like structure. The f3 pawn prevents any ...Ng4 or ...Nf5-e3 ideas and makes e4 rock-solid. This also prepares g4, which would stop Black's ...f5 break entirely. White's plan is clear: lock up the kingside, then steamroll on the queenside. This is the classical approach to fighting the KID.
kings-indian-defence.pawnStructure: White's f3 creates an ironclad e4 support but commits to a closed kingside structure
plans: Support e4 permanently, prepare queenside expansion with b4 and c5
Consider g4 to prevent ...f5
alternatives: Nd3 -- continuing the knight rerouting, also standard

kings-indian-defence.annotation: Black launches the kingside attack! This is THE move of the King's Indian Defense. The f5 pawn crashes into White's center, threatening ...f4 which would seal the kingside and create a devastating attack along the g-file after ...g5 and ...Rf6-g6. If White takes exf5, Black recaptures with ...gxf5 and gets an open g-file aimed at White's king. The race is officially on -- whoever gets there first wins. This is why the King's Indian is one of the most exciting openings in chess.
kings-indian-defence.pawnStructure: ...f5 creates the classic KID attack structure; after ...f4 Black gets a kingside pawn storm
plans: Play ...f4 to seal the kingside, then ...g5, ...h5, ...Rf6-g6 for a mating attack
If exf5, recapture ...gxf5 and use the open g-file
alternatives: a5 -- preventing White's b4, a slower approach but sometimes necessary

london-system.annotation: We open with d4, and in the London System, this is the only pawn move we commit to in the center for a while. The whole idea of the London is: set up a rock-solid structure with d4, Bf4, e3, Nf3, and Be2/Bd3 regardless of what Black does. It's a system opening -- you can play the same moves against almost anything.
london-system.pawnStructure: Single d4 pawn. The London aims for a d4-e3 pawn chain, not a broad d4-e4 center.
plans: Set up the London pyramid: d4, e3, Bf4
Develop all pieces to stable squares before pushing pawns
alternatives: Nf3 -- can transpose to the London on the next move

london-system.annotation: Black takes central space with d5. This is the most straightforward reply and leads to the main lines of the London. Black is saying: I'll match your center and we'll see who develops better. The d5 pawn also prevents e4, which means White's plan of Bf4 and e3 is natural rather than a concession.
london-system.pawnStructure: Symmetrical d4 vs d5. This will likely become a d4-e3 vs d5-e6 structure.
plans: Mirror White's central control
Develop naturally and look for breaks later
alternatives: Nf6 -- more flexible, can play into various Indian setups
f5 -- the Dutch, aggressive but risky

london-system.annotation: This IS the London System. We develop the bishop outside the pawn chain before playing e3. This is absolutely critical -- if we play e3 first, the bishop is stuck behind the pawns forever. The bishop on f4 controls the e5 square, which is our key outpost, and it's aimed at the b8-h2 diagonal. It also overprotects d2 and can drop back to g3 if challenged.
plans: Control the e5 square permanently
The bishop can retreat to g3 if challenged by Nh5
Support a future e4 or c4 break
alternatives: Nf3 -- develops but delays the bishop, risks getting it locked in after e3
c4 -- the Queen's Gambit, a completely different opening philosophy

london-system.annotation: Black develops the knight naturally. The knight on f6 fights for e4 and d5, and it's the most flexible development move. In some lines, Black might play Nh5 to challenge our bishop -- that's a key idea to be aware of. If Black does play Nh5, we retreat Bg3 and the knight is actually awkwardly placed on the rim.
plans: Develop naturally and prepare to challenge the Bf4
Consider Nh5 to trade the London bishop
alternatives: c5 -- immediately strikes at the center
Bf5 -- mirrors White's idea, interesting symmetry

london-system.annotation: We complete the London pyramid. The e3 pawn supports d4 and gives the light-squared bishop access to d3 or e2. Yes, it locks in the bishop on f4 behind the pawn chain, but that's exactly why we developed it first! The e3-d4 structure is incredibly solid and very hard for Black to break down.
london-system.pawnStructure: The London pyramid: d4-e3 with Bf4 already outside. This is the ideal structure. White will add c3 for extra support.
plans: Develop Bd3 or Be2 next
Add c3 to complete the fortress
Look for e4 or c4 breaks when the time is right
alternatives: Nf3 -- equally natural, just a move order question

london-system.annotation: Black mirrors with e6, creating the symmetrical pawn chain. The e6 pawn supports d5 and opens the diagonal for the dark-squared bishop. This is the most solid setup against the London, but like in the QGD, the c8 bishop is now hemmed in behind the e6 pawn. Black needs to find a way to activate it.
london-system.pawnStructure: Symmetric e3-d4 vs e6-d5. Both sides have stable, locked pawn chains. The fight will be about piece activity and pawn breaks.
plans: Develop the dark-squared bishop actively
Prepare c5 to challenge White's center
alternatives: c5 -- immediately challenges the center, more dynamic
Bg4 -- pins the knight but e3 isn't played yet so it's premature

london-system.annotation: We develop the knight to f3, completing the standard London setup of Bf4-e3-Nf3. The knight on f3 is rock solid -- it defends d4, eyes e5 (our dream square), and keeps h4 available for potential kingside plans. In the London, this piece almost never goes anywhere else.
plans: Support d4 and contest e5
Prepare Nbd2 and complete development
Keep h4 open for potential knight maneuvers
alternatives: c3 -- solidifies first, but the knight is more useful

london-system.annotation: Black strikes at our center. This is the most principled reaction to the London -- Black wants to challenge d4 and open the position. The c5 break is Black's main source of counterplay in these structures. If we take on c5, Black gets a nice open position. If we don't, the tension persists.
london-system.pawnStructure: The c5 push creates tension against d4. White must decide: maintain the tension (most common), take on c5, or push d5.
plans: Create tension against d4
If White takes, recapture with a piece and get active play
alternatives: Bd6 -- develops actively but c5 is more important strategically
b6 -- fianchetto, slower but solid

london-system.annotation: We add an extra defender to d4 with c3. This is the key London response to c5 -- rather than exchanging, we reinforce our center. Now d4 is defended three times (c3, e3, Nf3) and Black can't easily break through. The c3 pawn also takes away the b4 and d4 squares from Black's pieces.
london-system.pawnStructure: The c3-d4-e3 triangle is the London fortress. It's almost impossible for Black to break this down directly. White will play for a timely e4 or c4 break later.
plans: Maintain the rock-solid center
Develop Nbd2-e5 or prepare an e4 break
Keep the position closed and maneuver pieces to ideal squares
alternatives: dxc5 -- gives up the center, goes against London principles
Bb5+ -- checks but doesn't help the structure

london-system.annotation: Black develops the knight to pressure d4 and prepares to increase the central tension. The knight on c6 is well-placed, hitting d4 and e5. Black is building up forces against our center, which is the correct strategy -- apply pressure and wait for White to overextend.
plans: Increase pressure on d4
Prepare Bd6 to challenge the London bishop
alternatives: Nc6 is almost always the move here -- Nd7 blocks the bishop

london-system.annotation: We develop the knight to d2 rather than c3 because c3 is occupied by our pawn. The knight on d2 is a London trademark -- it supports e4, can reroute to f1-e5, and keeps the position flexible. It looks passive, but the knight is heading to useful squares. Think of Nbd2 as a launching pad.
plans: Reroute to e5 via Nbd2-f1-e5 or push e4 directly
Support a future e4 pawn break
Ne5 is the dream square for this knight
alternatives: Na3 -- rarely good, the knight is offside on the rim

london-system.annotation: Black places the bishop on d6, directly challenging our London bishop on f4. This is the critical moment -- Black is offering a trade. If we exchange Bxd6 Qxd6, Black gets easy development and the queen is active. If we retreat to g3, Black can consider Bxg3 hxg3 which gives us doubled pawns but also an open h-file. Both sides need to calculate carefully.
plans: Challenge the London bishop directly
After Bg3 Bxg3 hxg3, Black eliminates the bishop but opens the h-file for White
alternatives: Be7 -- safer but doesn't challenge the bishop
Qb6 -- hits b2 and d4 but allows Qb3 trades

london-system.annotation: We retreat to g3, which is the standard London response when Black challenges with Bd6. The bishop on g3 is still doing work -- it controls e5 and h4, and if Black trades with Bxg3, we get the h-file opened after hxg3. That open h-file can become a serious attacking weapon in the middlegame.
london-system.pawnStructure: If Black trades Bxg3 hxg3, White gets doubled g-pawns but an open h-file. This is actually favorable for White in many cases.
plans: If Bxg3 hxg3, use the open h-file for attack
If Black doesn't trade, the bishop stays active on g3
Prepare Bd3 and castle
alternatives: Bxd6 -- concedes the bishop pair and activates Black's queen
Be5 -- centralizes but can be exchanged easily

london-system.annotation: Black castles to safety. Smart timing -- before committing to the bishop trade on g3 or other plans, Black secures the king. In the London, the middlegame can get sharp if the h-file opens, so castling early is prudent. The rook also comes to f8 where it supports potential f5 or f6 ideas.
plans: Get the king safe before making structural decisions
Prepare b6-Bb7 or Qe7 with piece development
alternatives: Bxg3 -- can be played now or later, a strategic decision
Qe7 -- connects the rooks but delays castling

london-system.annotation: We develop the bishop to d3, a perfect square in the London. From d3, the bishop aims at the h7 pawn (a classic attacking motif), supports a potential e4 break, and pairs nicely with the queen on c2 or e2. Now we're ready to castle and our development is nearly complete. This is the London at its best -- every piece on a great square, rock-solid center, and ready for action.
plans: Aim at h7 with the Bd3+Qc2 battery
Castle kingside and prepare e4 or Qe2 plans
If the h-file opens after Bxg3, the battery becomes lethal
alternatives: Be2 -- safer but less ambitious, doesn't pressure h7

london-system.annotation: Black fianchettoes the queenside bishop. This is a very logical plan -- the c8 bishop has been stuck behind e6, and b6-Bb7 gives it a great diagonal. The bishop on b7 will hit e4 and d5, supporting Black's center from the flank. This also prepares Qc7 to contest the key diagonal.
london-system.pawnStructure: Black opens the b7 diagonal for the bishop. The b6 pawn also supports a potential c4 push in some lines.
plans: Fianchetto with Bb7 to activate the problem bishop
Prepare Qc7 targeting the g3 bishop and h2
Consider a5-a4 queenside expansion
alternatives: Bxg3 -- trades first before fianchettoing
Re8 -- improves the rook but the bishop development is more urgent

nimzo-indian.annotation: White opens with the queen's pawn, immediately staking a claim in the center and opening the diagonal for the dark-squared bishop. This is the starting move for all the major Indian defenses, and it's more flexible than e4 because the pawn is already defended by the queen.
nimzo-indian.pawnStructure: Single central pawn on d4; White aims to follow up with c4 for the ideal pawn center.
plans: Follow up with c4 to build a broad pawn center
Develop pieces behind the center pawns
alternatives: e4 — the other main first move, leading to more open positions

nimzo-indian.annotation: Black develops the knight to its most natural square, controlling e4 and d5. This is the hallmark of the Indian defenses — Black refuses to immediately commit pawns to the center, instead developing a piece first and keeping options open for how to challenge White's center later.
nimzo-indian.pawnStructure: No pawn commitments yet for Black; maximum flexibility retained.
plans: Control e4 to prevent White from playing e4 freely
Choose a defense system based on White's next move
alternatives: d5 — the classical approach, immediately contesting the center

nimzo-indian.annotation: White plays c4, the Queen's Gambit move, grabbing more space and aiming for the ideal d4+e4 pawn center. Together d4 and c4 control the critical d5 and e5 squares. The c-pawn also opens the diagonal for the queen and prepares Nc3.
nimzo-indian.pawnStructure: White pawns on c4 and d4 form the classic broad center. The c4 pawn eyes d5.
plans: Build the full center with e4 if allowed
Develop Nc3 to support the center
alternatives: Nf3 — a more restrained approach delaying c4

nimzo-indian.annotation: Black plays e6, preparing to develop the dark-squared bishop while maintaining control of d5. This is the key move order distinction — by playing e6 before d5, Black keeps the option of putting the bishop on b4 (Nimzo-Indian) or playing d5 later (Queen's Indian or QGD). It's all about flexibility.
nimzo-indian.pawnStructure: Black's e6 pawn supports a future d5 push and creates a solid but slightly cramped structure.
plans: Develop Bb4 pinning the knight if Nc3 is played
Play d5 later to challenge the center directly
alternatives: g6 — the King's Indian or Grunfeld approach with a fianchetto

nimzo-indian.annotation: White develops the knight to c3, the most natural square, defending e4 and pressuring d5. But here's the thing — Nc3 allows Black to enter the Nimzo-Indian with Bb4, which is one of the most respected defenses in chess. Some top players avoid Nc3 precisely for this reason.
nimzo-indian.pawnStructure: No change; the knight supports a future e4 advance.
plans: Support e4 to build the ideal center
Prepare to develop the remaining pieces
alternatives: Nf3 — avoids the Nimzo-Indian, can lead to Queen's Indian or Bogo-Indian

nimzo-indian.annotation: This is the defining move of the Nimzo-Indian Defense. Black pins the c3 knight to the king, which means White can't easily play e4. The whole point is that Black disrupts White's plan for a broad pawn center without having to commit a pawn to d5 yet. Nimzowitsch's idea was brilliant — use a piece to control the center instead of pawns.
nimzo-indian.pawnStructure: Still flexible for Black; no central pawn committed yet beyond e6.
plans: Maintain the pin to prevent e4
Castle quickly and then decide on the central pawn structure
alternatives: d5 — transposes to the Queen's Gambit Declined
b6 — the Queen's Indian Defense

nimzo-indian.annotation: The Classical variation — White places the queen on c2 for several reasons. First, it prevents Black from doubling White's pawns with Bxc3. Second, the queen supports a future e4 push. Third, it keeps the pawn structure intact, which is a major strategic consideration in the Nimzo-Indian.
nimzo-indian.pawnStructure: White preserves the option of a clean pawn structure by discouraging Bxc3+.
plans: Push e4 with queen support
Develop pieces while maintaining structural integrity
alternatives: e3 — the Rubinstein variation, accepting doubled pawns may come
a3 — immediately asking the bishop what it wants to do

nimzo-indian.annotation: Black castles, getting the king to safety before deciding on the central pawn structure. This is a very principled approach — you don't want to be caught in the center when the position opens up. By castling first, Black can then choose between d5, c5, or even d6 setups depending on what White does next.
plans: Prepare central action with d5 or c5
Complete development and connect rooks
alternatives: d5 — immediately challenging the center before castling
c5 — striking at d4 right away

nimzo-indian.annotation: White finally asks the question: what are you going to do with that bishop? The bishop on b4 has to make a decision. It can retreat, but then White has gained a tempo and the a3 pawn can be useful for a b4 expansion later. White is essentially calling Black's bluff on the pin.
nimzo-indian.pawnStructure: The a3 pawn prepares a potential b4 advance and gains the bishop pair.
plans: Win the bishop pair after Bxc3
Use a3-b4 to expand on the queenside
alternatives: e4 — aggressively pushing for the center immediately
Nf3 — continuing development

nimzo-indian.annotation: Black takes on c3, giving White doubled pawns. This is the fundamental trade-off of the Nimzo-Indian — Black gives up the bishop pair, but in return White's pawn structure is damaged and the doubled c-pawns can become a long-term weakness. The check forces the queen to recapture.
nimzo-indian.pawnStructure: After Qxc3, White will have doubled c-pawns (c3 and c4), which are a structural weakness but also control important central squares.
plans: Target the doubled c-pawns in the endgame
Play d5 to establish a central presence
alternatives: Bf8 — retreating, but this loses too much time after a3

nimzo-indian.annotation: White recaptures with the queen rather than the b-pawn. Taking with the queen keeps the c-file pawn structure cleaner — well, relatively — and places the queen actively on c3 where it eyes the kingside along the diagonal and controls e5. The queen is well-placed here, but it could become a target.
nimzo-indian.pawnStructure: White has pawns on a3 and c4 on the queenside; the doubled pawns are avoided but the c3 queen may need to relocate.
plans: Develop rapidly and push e4
Use the bishop pair in an open position
alternatives: bxc3 — accepting doubled pawns but keeping the queen flexible

nimzo-indian.annotation: Now Black strikes in the center with d5. The timing is perfect — White spent time with Qc2, a3, and Qxc3, so Black has completed development and castled. Now d5 challenges the c4 pawn directly and fights for central control. This is the classic Nimzo strategy: disrupt White's center, then plant your own pawn there.
nimzo-indian.pawnStructure: Black establishes a strong pawn on d5 challenging White's c4. A symmetric-looking center but with dynamic tension.
plans: Exchange on c4 and target the isolated or hanging pawns
Develop the queenside pieces actively
alternatives: b6 — fianchettoing the bishop for a slower approach
c5 — attacking d4 instead

nimzo-indian.annotation: White develops the knight to its natural square, preparing to castle and supporting the center. The knight on f3 is solid — it controls e5 and d4, and helps White complete kingside development. White needs to decide how to handle the tension in the center: take on d5, or maintain it.
plans: Castle kingside and complete development
Decide on central pawn tension — take on d5 or play e3
alternatives: Bg5 — pinning the knight first before developing the kingside knight

nimzo-indian.annotation: Black takes on c4, resolving the central tension in a very concrete way. This isn't about winning a pawn — White will get it back — it's about opening the position and creating targets. After White recaptures, Black can develop the bishop to a6 or b7 and put pressure on the open files and diagonals.
nimzo-indian.pawnStructure: The center is now open. White will recapture on c4, leading to a position where piece activity matters more than pawn structure.
plans: Develop the light-squared bishop actively to a6 or b7
Use the semi-open c-file and d-file for rooks
alternatives: b6 — developing the bishop before capturing
c5 — maintaining more tension

nimzo-indian.annotation: White recaptures the pawn with the queen. The queen on c4 is active but potentially exposed — Black can gain tempi by attacking it. This is a key dynamic in many Nimzo-Indian lines: White gets the bishop pair and an active position, but Black gets time and targets.
nimzo-indian.pawnStructure: Symmetric center with d4 vs. nothing — White has the central pawn majority potential with e4.
plans: Push e4 to seize the center
Develop the bishops actively using the bishop pair advantage
alternatives: e3 — a more modest recapture keeping the queen on c3

nimzo-indian.annotation: Black prepares to fianchetto the light-squared bishop or develop it to a6. The move b6 is multipurpose — it supports c5 to hit the d4 pawn, and the long diagonal b7-g2 can be very powerful if the bishop lands there. But here Black has a more specific idea: Ba6, hitting the queen on c4 directly.
nimzo-indian.pawnStructure: Black's b6 prepares a queenside fianchetto and supports the c5 break.
plans: Develop the bishop to a6 attacking the c4 queen
Play c5 to challenge d4
alternatives: c5 — immediately striking at d4
Nbd7 — developing a piece first

nimzo-indian.annotation: White develops the bishop aggressively, pinning the f6 knight against the queen. This is a very active square for the bishop — it creates real pressure on the kingside and makes Black think carefully about pawn structure. If Black plays h6, the bishop can retreat to h4 maintaining the pin, or exchange on f6 damaging Black's kingside.
plans: Maintain pressure on f6 and the kingside
Use the pin to support e4
alternatives: e3 — a more solid developing move
Bf4 — developing without creating a pin

nimzo-indian.annotation: Black develops the bishop to a6 with tempo, attacking the queen on c4. This is the whole point of the b6 + Ba6 setup in the Nimzo-Indian — the bishop is very active on a6, hitting c4 and e2, and it's hard for White to find a comfortable square for the queen. Black gains time and disrupts White's coordination.
plans: Harass the White queen and slow down White's development
Prepare c5 to complete the queenside pawn structure
alternatives: Bb7 — fianchettoing to the long diagonal instead

nimzo-indian.annotation: The queen retreats to a4, getting out of the bishop's line while maintaining some activity. From a4, the queen eyes the a-file and keeps some pressure on the queenside. It's not the most comfortable square, but the alternatives aren't great either — the a6 bishop really is annoying.
plans: Regroup and push e4 to seize the center
Potentially reroute the queen to a better square later
alternatives: Qc2 — returning to the original square
Qb3 — keeping central pressure

nimzo-indian.annotation: Black strikes at d4, the key break in the Nimzo-Indian. This move challenges White's central pawn and opens the position for Black's pieces. Combined with the active bishop on a6 and the pressure on the center, Black achieves a very comfortable position. The Nimzo-Indian has delivered exactly what Black wanted: an active, flexible middlegame with no structural weaknesses.
nimzo-indian.pawnStructure: Black's c5 challenges the d4 pawn, creating central tension. If dxc5, Black recaptures with excellent piece activity.
plans: Increase central pressure after the pawn exchange
Activate rooks on the c-file and d-file
alternatives: Nbd7 — developing first before the central break
Rc8 — preparing c5 with rook support

old-indian-defence.annotation: White opens with the queen's pawn, establishing central presence and preparing the classic d4+c4 pawn duo. This sets the stage for a strategic game where central control and piece coordination matter more than early tactics.
plans: Follow up with c4 for a broad center
Develop pieces systematically
alternatives: e4 — more open, tactical games
Nf3 — delays commitment

old-indian-defence.annotation: Black develops the knight to its most natural square, controlling e4 and maintaining maximum flexibility. From here Black can go into dozens of different defenses depending on how White continues. The knight on f6 is the universal response to 1.d4.
plans: Control e4 and react to White's setup
Keep all defensive options open
alternatives: d5 — immediately contests the center

old-indian-defence.annotation: White grabs queenside space and controls d5 with the c-pawn. The d4+c4 duo is the most ambitious setup against Indian defenses. White is asserting dominance over the center and daring Black to challenge it directly or fight from the flanks.
old-indian-defence.pawnStructure: The d4+c4 formation gives White a space advantage and central control.
plans: Support the center with Nc3
Push e4 if Black allows it
alternatives: Nf3 — more flexible, delays c4

old-indian-defence.annotation: This is the signature move of the Old Indian Defence. Instead of the modern g6 (King's Indian) or e6 (Nimzo/Queen's Indian), Black plays d6 — a solid, old-school approach that keeps the center closed and prepares e5. The Old Indian is about building a fortress and then striking when the time is right. It's less theoretical than the King's Indian but shares similar counterattacking DNA.
old-indian-defence.pawnStructure: The d6 pawn supports a future e5 advance and keeps the center compact.
plans: Prepare the e5 break to challenge White's center
Develop solidly before committing to a plan
alternatives: g6 — King's Indian, more popular and aggressive
e6 — Queen's Indian/Nimzo territory

old-indian-defence.annotation: White develops the knight to its ideal square, reinforcing control of d5 and e4. The knight on c3 is the backbone of White's central strategy — it supports the e4 push and keeps a grip on d5. This is the most natural developing move in any Queen's Pawn game.
plans: Support e4 to build a massive center
Maintain d5 control
alternatives: Nf3 — also natural, but Nc3 puts more immediate pressure on the center

old-indian-defence.annotation: Black develops the knight to d7 rather than c6. Why d7 instead of c6? Because in the Old Indian, Black wants to play e5, and a knight on c6 would block the c-pawn from supporting the center. The d7 knight supports e5, can reroute to f8-e6 or c5, and keeps the c-pawn free for a future c6 or c5 break. It's a quiet move with deep positional logic.
plans: Support the e5 advance
Reroute the knight to c5 or f8 depending on the position
alternatives: c6 — possible but blocks the c-pawn and commits to a different structure

old-indian-defence.annotation: White seizes the full center with e4, achieving the ideal d4+e4+c4 pawn trio. This is exactly what White wants in the Old Indian — a massive spatial advantage. The center is now a wall, and White controls virtually every important central square. Black needs to find the right moment to strike at this center, or it will slowly suffocate.
old-indian-defence.pawnStructure: White's d4+e4+c4 is the classical broad center, dominating the board. Black must challenge it or be squeezed.
plans: Use the space advantage to develop an attack
Prepare f3 and Be3 for a complete setup
alternatives: Nf3 — more modest, but White should seize the center when given the chance

old-indian-defence.annotation: Black strikes back at the center immediately — this is the whole point of the Old Indian. The e5 push challenges White's d4 pawn directly and fights for breathing room. If White takes on e5, Black recaptures with the knight and gets active piece play. If White doesn't take, the center becomes locked and the game turns into a strategic battle where Black has clear counterplay on the kingside.
old-indian-defence.pawnStructure: After e5, the center tension determines the entire middlegame character. d4+e5 creates a locked or semi-locked structure.
plans: Challenge White's broad center
If the center locks, launch a kingside attack
alternatives: g6 — transposes to a King's Indian setup, but e5 is the Old Indian's identity

old-indian-defence.annotation: White develops the knight and supports d4 before the tension in the center is resolved. The knight on f3 is the natural defender of d4 and also controls the e5 square. White is content to maintain the tension — there's no rush to resolve things because the space advantage means White can develop freely while Black is cramped.
plans: Maintain central tension
Complete development with Be2 and O-O
alternatives: d5 — closes the center immediately, leading to a King's Indian-style pawn race

old-indian-defence.annotation: Black develops the bishop to e7, a solid and flexible square. In the Old Indian, the bishop on e7 isn't as aggressive as g7 in the King's Indian, but it serves important defensive duties — it covers d6, supports castling, and can later swing to f6 or d8 depending on the pawn structure. This is the modest, reliable approach that defines the Old Indian philosophy.
plans: Castle kingside quickly
Reposition the bishop based on how the center resolves
alternatives: g6 — fianchetto, but that's really the King's Indian at this point

old-indian-defence.annotation: White develops the bishop to e2, a safe and flexible square that prepares castling. The bishop on e2 doesn't commit to an aggressive diagonal yet — it can later go to d3 or f3 if needed. This is the classical approach: complete development, castle, then decide on a concrete plan based on how Black reacts.
plans: Castle kingside immediately
Later decide between f3+g4 kingside plans or queenside expansion
alternatives: Bd3 — more aggressive, but can be hit by Nc5 later

old-indian-defence.annotation: Black castles to safety. With the king secured, Black can now focus on the middlegame plan. In the Old Indian, the kingside castle is standard — the king is safe behind the e7 bishop, and Black can start thinking about when and how to break in the center with c6 or on the kingside with f5.
plans: Prepare a central or kingside break
Activate the rooks

old-indian-defence.annotation: White castles, completing the basic development. Both sides are now fully castled with pieces on natural squares. The real strategic battle begins now. White has more space but needs a concrete plan, while Black is compact and solid, waiting for the right moment to strike. This is where understanding plans matters more than moves.
plans: Choose between queenside expansion (b4) or kingside attack (f4)
Use the space advantage to create threats

old-indian-defence.annotation: Black prepares the key d5 break by supporting it with the c-pawn. This is the most important strategic idea in the Old Indian — Black needs to challenge the center with d5 at some point, and c6 is the necessary preparation. Without this break, Black would slowly suffocate under White's space advantage. The c6 move also gives the d7 knight the option of going to c5 via b6.
old-indian-defence.pawnStructure: c6 prepares d5, which would liquidate the center and free Black's pieces.
plans: Play d5 at the right moment to break open the center
Redirect the knight via b6 to c5 or a4
alternatives: Re8 — preparing the break more slowly
a5 — preventing b4 queenside expansion

old-indian-defence.annotation: White closes the center with d5, a critical strategic decision. By pushing d5, White locks the center and the game becomes a race: White attacks on the queenside with a4-a5-b4, while Black attacks on the kingside with f5. This is the classic King's Indian/Old Indian pawn structure where both sides play on opposite flanks. White's space advantage on the queenside is balanced by Black's kingside counterplay.
old-indian-defence.pawnStructure: The closed d5-e4 vs e5-d6 structure creates a clear strategic plan for both sides: White pushes queenside, Black pushes kingside.
plans: Launch queenside operations with a4, b4, c5
Use the space advantage to break through on the a and b files
alternatives: dxe5 — opens the position but gives Black active pieces
Be3 — maintaining tension a bit longer

old-indian-defence.annotation: Black locks down the queenside with c5, preventing White's b4 expansion and creating a permanent pawn wedge. This is a crucial positional decision — by playing c5, Black says 'I'm not going to let you attack on the queenside. Now let's see who's faster on the kingside.' The pawn on c5 also gives Black control of the d4 square and creates a potential outpost there. The game is now a pure flanking battle.
old-indian-defence.pawnStructure: The c5+d6+e5 chain is rock solid, blocking White's queenside ambitions and directing all the action to the kingside.
plans: Launch a kingside attack with f5, g5, Nf8-g6
Use the d4 square as an outpost for a knight
alternatives: Nc5 — active piece play, but c5 is the more principled structural move

petrov-defence.annotation: The King's Pawn opening. We grab the center immediately and open diagonals for our bishop and queen. This is the most classical first move in chess -- direct, principled, and it puts the question to Black right away: how are you going to contest the center?
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: Single e4 pawn controls d5 and f5 squares.
plans: Develop quickly and fight for central control
Open lines for the king's bishop
alternatives: d4 -- equally strong, leads to closed/semi-closed games
Nf3 -- flexible, delays pawn commitment

petrov-defence.annotation: Black mirrors us with the most principled reply. By playing e5, Black stakes an equal claim in the center and opens lines for their own dark-squared bishop. This is the starting point of all Open Games and tells us Black wants a fair, symmetrical fight.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: Symmetrical e4 vs e5 pawn center. Both sides control key central squares.
plans: Maintain central pawn tension
Develop kingside pieces quickly
alternatives: c5 -- the Sicilian, asymmetric and combative
e6 -- the French, solid but slightly passive

petrov-defence.annotation: We develop the knight to its best square, attacking the e5 pawn immediately. This is the most natural move -- the knight goes to f3 in almost every Open Game. We're developing with tempo, forcing Black to make a decision about how to defend that e5 pawn right now.
plans: Attack e5 to gain a tempo advantage
Prepare d4 to build a broad pawn center
alternatives: Bc4 -- the Italian/Bishop's Opening, develops but doesn't pressure e5 directly

petrov-defence.annotation: This is the Petrov Defence. Instead of defending the e5 pawn, Black counterattacks our e4 pawn. The logic is: 'You're threatening my pawn? Fine, I'm threatening yours.' It's a deeply symmetrical, solid approach. The Petrov has a reputation for being drawish at the top level, but the positions require precise play from both sides.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: Still symmetrical. Neither side has committed to a specific pawn structure yet.
plans: Counterattack rather than defend -- a philosophical choice by Black
Aim for simplification and equality
alternatives: Nc6 -- the most common defense, keeps more tension
d6 -- Philidor's Defence, solid but passive

petrov-defence.annotation: We grab the pawn. This is the critical test of the Petrov -- if we don't take, Black just has a comfortable, equal position. The point is that Nxe4 by Black right now would be a mistake because after d4, our center is massive and Black's knight is unstable. So Black has to be careful here.
plans: Win a pawn temporarily to disrupt Black's coordination
After Black recaptures, build a strong center with d4
alternatives: Nc3 -- the Three Knights / Four Knights, solid but less testing

petrov-defence.annotation: Black kicks our knight back. This is the Classical Petrov -- the most respected continuation. Black doesn't fall for the trap of Nxe4 immediately. The move d6 forces our knight to retreat and prepares to recapture on e4 only after we've moved away. It's patient, precise chess.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: Black's d6 pawn creates a small pawn chain pointing toward e5. This will dissolve once the knight moves.
plans: Force the knight back, then recapture on e4
Reach a symmetrical position with equal chances
alternatives: Qe7 -- pins the knight but blocks the bishop, slightly awkward

petrov-defence.annotation: We retreat to f3, the natural square. The knight was always coming back here -- the whole Nxe5 adventure was about gaining time and disrupting Black's development sequence. Now we're back on f3 with the idea of playing d4 next to build a real center.
plans: Play d4 next to establish a classical pawn center
Develop pieces rapidly toward the center
alternatives: Nc4 -- unusual, the knight is offside on c4

petrov-defence.annotation: Now Black takes on e4. The timing is right because our knight is no longer on e5 threatening things. We've reached the main tabiya of the Classical Petrov. Black has temporarily won a pawn, but we'll win it back with d4 -- the position is heading toward equality, but there's still plenty of chess to play.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: The e-file is about to become fully open once we play d4 and Black's knight moves.
plans: Win the pawn back with d4 hitting the d6 pawn
Use the semi-open e-file for rook activity
alternatives: Be7 -- too passive, lets White keep a comfortable edge after d4

petrov-defence.annotation: This is the key move. We establish a classical pawn center with d4, and we're hitting Black's d6 pawn which was supporting the knight. Black will have to figure out what to do with the knight on e4 -- it can't stay there forever. The d4-pawn also opens up our dark-squared bishop.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: White has a broad d4 center. Black needs to equalize by challenging it or completing development.
plans: Develop Bd3 to pressure the e4 knight
Castle quickly and use the open center to our advantage
alternatives: Bd3 -- developing first, but d4 is more direct and principled

petrov-defence.annotation: Black solidifies. The pawn on d5 supports the knight on e4 and creates a symmetrical pawn structure. This is the hallmark of the Petrov -- both sides have a pawn on d4/d5, and the position is balanced. But don't be fooled by the symmetry; there are still real decisions about piece placement and plans.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: Symmetrical d4 vs d5. The center is locked and neither side has a pawn majority yet. This structure often leads to minority attacks or piece play.
plans: Maintain the solid d5 outpost for pieces
Develop smoothly and prepare to challenge White's initiative
alternatives: Be7 -- allows Bd3 with tempo, less accurate

petrov-defence.annotation: We develop the bishop to its ideal diagonal, directly targeting the knight on e4. This is the most natural square -- d3 puts the bishop on the b1-h7 diagonal, which often becomes relevant for kingside attacks later. We're also clearing the back rank for castling.
plans: Pressure the e4 knight and the h7 square
Castle kingside quickly
Consider c4 later to challenge Black's d5 pawn
alternatives: Qe2 -- pins the knight but blocks the bishop development

petrov-defence.annotation: Black develops the knight to its most natural square. The knight on c6 puts pressure on d4, our center pawn, and supports the knight on e4 indirectly. It's logical development -- get the pieces out to good squares. Black is also preparing to develop the dark-squared bishop.
plans: Pressure the d4 pawn
Prepare Be7 and castle kingside
alternatives: Be7 -- equally good, a matter of move order
Nd7 -- supports e4 knight but blocks the c8 bishop

petrov-defence.annotation: We castle immediately. King safety first. In the Petrov, the center is semi-open and things can get tactical quickly, so getting the king tucked away is essential. We also connect the rooks and prepare to use the e1 square for our rook, putting pressure down the e-file.
plans: Activate the f1 rook on the e-file
Prepare Re1 to increase central pressure
Consider c4 to challenge Black's d5 pawn
alternatives: c4 -- aggressive but premature before castling

petrov-defence.annotation: Black develops the bishop modestly to e7. This is very typical of the Petrov -- Black isn't trying to be flashy. The bishop on e7 supports castling, covers some key squares, and can potentially redeploy later. It's a solid, professional choice over the more aggressive Bd6 which would block the d-pawn.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: No structural change. Black is completing development before making pawn commitments.
plans: Castle kingside and reach a safe, solid position
Consider Bf6 later to put pressure on d4
alternatives: Bd6 -- more active but blocks the d-pawn and can be hit by c4
Bg4 -- pins the knight but can be challenged by h3

petrov-defence.annotation: Now we strike at the center. The c4 push challenges Black's d5 pawn directly and is the main way to create tension in the Petrov. If Black takes on c4, we recapture with the bishop and get a nice open position. If they don't take, we can exchange ourselves and create an isolated queen pawn position that gives us active piece play.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: We're attacking the d5 base. If dxc4, Bxc4 gives us the open d-file and bishop pair ideas. If Black maintains d5, cxd5 creates an IQP structure.
plans: Open the position to exploit our lead in development
Create pressure against d5 or gain the bishop pair
alternatives: Re1 -- solid, improves the rook first
h3 -- prophylactic, prevents Bg4

petrov-defence.annotation: Black plays actively, jumping to b4 to target our d3 bishop. The idea is to force the bishop to move and potentially win time. The knight on b4 also eyes the c2 and d3 squares. This is a concrete approach -- Black is trying to disrupt our smooth development rather than just passively completing their own.
plans: Harass the Bd3 and gain time
Potentially land the knight on d3 or c2 if White is careless
alternatives: Nf6 -- retreats to safety but concedes ground
dxc4 -- simplifies the center

petrov-defence.annotation: We retreat the bishop to e2 -- a modest but correct square. The bishop on e2 stays on a useful diagonal and doesn't block anything. We could have gone to c2 or e4, but e2 is the most flexible. From e2, the bishop can support both flanks and we maintain our pawn tension with c4 vs d5.
plans: Maintain pressure with the c4 pawn
Prepare a3 to kick the knight from b4 if needed
Re1 and central pressure
alternatives: Bc2 -- keeps the bishop active but the knight on b4 is annoying there
cxd5 -- resolves tension but gives Black easy play

petrov-defence.annotation: Black castles, getting the king to safety. This is the right moment -- the pieces are developed enough and the king needs to be out of the center before the position opens further. With both sides castled, the game transitions into a middlegame battle where White has a slight space advantage from d4 and Black has solid piece placement.
petrov-defence.pawnStructure: The c4 vs d5 tension remains the key structural question. Resolution will define the middlegame character.
plans: Complete development and prepare for the middlegame
The knight on b4 may need to find a better home soon
alternatives: Bf5 -- develops the bishop before castling, but king safety takes priority

philidor-defence.annotation: White opens with the king's pawn, claiming the center and opening lines for the bishop and queen. The most popular first move, setting the stage for open games.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: Single e4 pawn controls d5 and f5. White plans to follow with Nf3 and d4.
plans: Develop quickly with Nf3 and d4 for a classical center
Open lines for rapid piece activity

philidor-defence.annotation: Black mirrors White's approach, creating a symmetrical center. This is the most direct response — Black immediately fights for the same central squares. The e5 pawn controls d4 and f4, preventing White from dominating unchallenged.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: Symmetrical e4 vs e5. The battle for d4 and d5 will determine the middlegame.
plans: Defend e5 and develop pieces naturally
Look for ...d5 break when prepared
alternatives: 1...c5 (Sicilian) — asymmetrical and more combative
1...e6 (French) — solid but locks in the light bishop

philidor-defence.annotation: White develops the knight to its ideal square, attacking e5. The knight controls d4 and helps prepare d4 to open the center. This is the most natural move — develop with purpose, attack something, and prepare to castle.
plans: Attack e5 and prepare d4
Castle kingside quickly

philidor-defence.annotation: The Philidor Defense! Instead of the more common 2...Nc6, Black supports e5 with the d-pawn. The philosophy is defensive: build a solid, compact structure and avoid early tactical complications. The downside is clear — the dark-squared bishop is blocked behind d6. Philidor called this the best defense in his 1749 treatise, but modern players find it a bit passive.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: The d6-e5 chain is very solid but slightly cramped. Black has a 'small center' that's hard to attack but limits piece mobility, especially the dark-squared bishop.
plans: Develop ...Nf6 and ...Be7, keeping the position solid
Eventually free the position with ...d5 or ...f5
alternatives: 2...Nc6 — the more popular choice, develops a piece and defends e5 actively
2...d5 — the Petroff-like Philidor Counter-Gambit, very rare

philidor-defence.annotation: White immediately challenges the center, which is the right approach against the Philidor. By pushing d4, White forces Black to make a decision: exchange on d4 (opening the position when Black is cramped), maintain the tension, or push ...exd4. White wants to open the center where the space advantage matters most.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: Central tension between d4/e4 and d6/e5. White's center is aggressive and space-gaining.
plans: Open the center to exploit the space advantage
Develop pieces behind the pawns and attack
alternatives: 3.Bc4 — develops first, less direct but still strong
3.d3 — quiet, leads to a King's Indian Attack setup

philidor-defence.annotation: Black develops the knight to f6, the most natural square. It attacks e4, prepares castling, and keeps the position flexible. Black doesn't capture on d4 yet because that would open the center when Black's development is lagging — instead, Black maintains the tension and develops pieces.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: The tension in the center remains. Black's knight on f6 pressures e4, adding to the central fight.
plans: Complete development with ...Be7 and ...O-O
Maintain the central tension as long as possible
alternatives: 3...exd4 — releases the tension but opens lines when Black is less developed
3...Nd7 — the Hanham Variation, very solid but more passive

philidor-defence.annotation: White develops the knight to c3, supporting e4 and controlling d5. The knight on c3 is essential — it keeps the center intact and prepares for further development. With both knights out, White is ready to develop the bishops and castle. The position is becoming a standard Philidor structure.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: White's center is well-supported. The d4-e4 complex is strong and Black must find a way to challenge it.
plans: Develop Bc4 or Be2 and castle
Maintain central pressure and prepare for middlegame

philidor-defence.annotation: Black develops the knight to d7 rather than c6. This is the Hanham Variation approach within the Philidor — the knight on d7 supports e5 and keeps the c-pawn free to advance later with ...c6 or ...c5. On c6, the knight would block the c-pawn and also be tactically exposed to d5 advances. The d7 knight is humble but functional.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: Black's structure remains compact: d6-e5 with knights on f6 and d7. The c-pawn is free for ...c6 or ...c5.
plans: Play ...Be7 and ...O-O for solid development
Prepare ...c6 followed by ...d5 to free the position
alternatives: 4...Nc6 — more active but blocks the c-pawn and can be hit by d5

philidor-defence.annotation: White puts the bishop on its most aggressive diagonal, targeting f7 — the weakest square in Black's position. The bishop on c4 combines with the knight on f3 to create real threats, and it also supports a potential d5 push. This is the most principled development — active pieces aimed at Black's weak points.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: No structural change. The bishop on c4 creates tactical pressure on f7 and supports central advances.
plans: Castle and create threats against f7
Prepare d5 or dxe5 to open lines
alternatives: 5.Be2 — solid but less ambitious, doesn't pressure f7

philidor-defence.annotation: Black develops the bishop to e7 — modest but necessary. The bishop on e7 prepares castling and covers the d6-b4 diagonal. In the Philidor, Black doesn't get flashy bishop placements; the compensation is the rock-solid structure. The bishop on e7 is passive compared to Bc5 or Bb4, but it's the most reliable in this cramped position.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: No change. Black's setup is compact: d6-e5, knights on d7 and f6, bishop on e7.
plans: Castle immediately and get the king safe
Prepare ...c6 and eventually ...d5 to free the position
alternatives: 5...Bb4 — more aggressive pin but can be resolved with a3

philidor-defence.annotation: White castles, completing kingside development. The king is safe, the rook is now on f1 supporting f4 ideas, and White has a clear development advantage. White can now focus entirely on increasing the pressure — the question is whether to play for d5, f4, or a slower buildup.
plans: Play Re1 to support e4 and prepare for central action
Consider dxe5 dxe5 followed by central pressure on the open d-file

philidor-defence.annotation: Black castles, finally getting the king to safety. The position is now a typical Philidor middlegame: White has more space and slightly more active pieces, Black has a solid structure and no weaknesses. Black needs to find a way to free the position — usually with ...c6 and ...d5, or ...exd4 followed by activity.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: Both sides are castled. The d4-e4 vs d6-e5 tension is the defining feature of the position.
plans: Play ...c6 to prepare ...d5
Alternatively play ...Re8 and ...Bf8 to regroup

philidor-defence.annotation: White places the rook on e1, reinforcing the e4 pawn and preparing for central action. The rook on e1 is the most natural square — it supports e4, controls the e-file, and prepares for any central opening. If the center opens after dxe5 dxe5, the rook is already perfectly placed on the half-open file.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: No change. The rook supports the central structure from behind.
plans: Prepare dxe5 to open the d-file if advantageous
Play a4 to gain space on the queenside and restrict ...b5
alternatives: 7.d5 — closes the center, changes the character completely

philidor-defence.annotation: Black plays ...c6, the key preparatory move for the liberating ...d5 push. Without ...c6 first, ...d5 would leave the d5 pawn unsupported and easy to attack. The c6 pawn also controls d5, preventing any White knight from landing there. This is patient chess — Black is slowly building toward the freeing break.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: The c6 pawn completes Black's compact structure. Black is now one move from ...d5, which would equalize the center.
plans: Play ...d5 to challenge the center and free the pieces
If ...d5 isn't possible yet, play ...Qc7 and ...Rd8 to prepare it

philidor-defence.annotation: White plays a4, a typical move to grab queenside space and prevent ...b5. The pawn on a4 also controls b5, making it harder for Black's knight to reroute via b6. More importantly, a4 prepares Ba2 or a5, creating queenside pressure. White is playing on both flanks while maintaining the center.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: The a4 pawn claims queenside space. White has a spatial advantage on both wings now, squeezing Black's position.
plans: Play a5 to further restrict Black on the queenside
Maintain central tension and look for the right moment to open the position
alternatives: 8.d5 — aggressive central closure, leads to a different type of game

philidor-defence.annotation: Black plays ...b6, preparing to fianchetto the queen's bishop to b7. This is a practical solution to the Philidor's biggest problem — the light-squared bishop has been doing nothing. From b7, it will support ...d5 and have some diagonal activity. The move ...b6 also prepares ...Bb7 and ...a6 for a compact queenside setup.
philidor-defence.pawnStructure: Black's b6 creates a slight weakness on c6 and a6, but the benefit of activating the bishop outweighs this. The structure is b6-c6-d6-e5.
plans: Fianchetto with ...Bb7 to activate the light-squared bishop
Prepare ...d5 with all pieces supporting the break
alternatives: 8...Qc7 — prepares ...d5 more directly but the bishop stays passive

pirc-defence.annotation: White seizes the center with the king's pawn, claiming d5 and f5 and opening lines for the bishop and queen. The most popular opening move — it establishes a foothold that Black must address immediately.
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: Single e4 pawn. White plans d4 next for a commanding two-pawn center.
plans: Build a d4-e4 pawn center
Develop pieces rapidly behind the strong center
alternatives: 1.d4 — leads to queen's pawn openings, avoids the Pirc entirely

pirc-defence.annotation: The Pirc Defense starts here. Black plays ...d6 instead of challenging the center immediately, saying 'go ahead, build your big center — I'll undermine it later.' This is a hypermodern approach: Black lets White overextend and then strikes from the flanks. The d6 pawn controls e5 and prepares a kingside fianchetto.
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: Black's d6 pawn is modest but flexible. It controls e5 and supports a later ...e5 or ...c5 break depending on how White sets up.
plans: Fianchetto the bishop to g7 to pressure White's center from the flank
Prepare ...e5 or ...c5 to challenge the center when the time is right
alternatives: 1...e5 — the most direct reply, immediately fighting for the center
1...c5 (Sicilian) — creates asymmetry and fights for d4

pirc-defence.annotation: White doesn't hesitate — two pawns in the center, maximum space. The d4-e4 duo controls everything: c5, d5, e5, f5. Against the Pirc, this is practically automatic. White will try to use this space advantage to develop quickly and launch an attack before Black can chip away at the center.
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: The classical d4-e4 center gives White significant space advantage. The risk is that this big center can become a target if Black challenges it effectively with ...c5 or ...e5.
plans: Develop pieces behind the center and prepare a kingside attack
Push f4 to build an even bigger center and threaten f5

pirc-defence.annotation: Black develops the knight to f6, pressuring e4 and continuing the hypermodern strategy. The knight on f6 is flexible — it eyes e4 and d5, and doesn't commit to a particular pawn structure yet. Black is saying: 'I'll develop pieces first, challenge your center second.'
pirc-defence.pawnStructure: No pawn change. Black keeps maximum flexibility for the structure.
plans: Fianchetto with ...g6 and ...Bg7 next
Castle kingside quickly and then attack the center
